title: Nearby Search (Yelp / Maps) — System Design
description: Design a proximity-search service: given a user's location, return the closest matching places (restaurants, shops) fast. The core problem is indexing 2D geography so 'find everything within radius R' is a cheap lookup instead of a full scan.

nodes.label: Mobile / Web Client
nodes.description: User searching for places near a location.

nodes.label: Load Balancer / API GW
nodes.description: Routes incoming search and write requests; enforces abuse controls at the edge — coordinate validation, max radius, max limit, and per-client/API-key rate limits — and routes reads to the nearest regional/cell-prefix index.

nodes.label: Nearby Search Service
nodes.description: Resolves a lat/lng + radius into nearby place ids, then ranks.

nodes.label: Geo Index
nodes.description: Spatial index (geohash / quadtree / S2) mapping cells to place ids.

nodes.label: Place Service
nodes.description: CRUD and reads of place details/metadata.

nodes.label: Place Store
nodes.description: Authoritative place records (name, location, category, hours).

nodes.label: Place Cache
nodes.description: Hot place details hydrated into search results.

nodes.label: Ranking Service
nodes.description: Scores candidates by distance, rating, popularity, and filters.

nodes.label: Index Builder
nodes.description: Consumes place changes and updates the geo index cells.

nodes.label: Place Change Queue
nodes.description: Streams place create/update/delete events to the indexer.

nodes.label: Routing / ETA Service
nodes.description: Optional: computes travel distance/ETA for ranking and display.

functional: Given a location (lat/lng) and radius, return places within it.
Rank results by a blend of distance, rating, and popularity.
Add / update / remove places; results reflect changes within minutes.
Return place details (name, address, hours, rating) for results.
nonFunctional: Read-heavy: searches vastly outnumber place updates.
Low latency: search p99 < 200 ms including ranking.
Scale to ~100M places worldwide and high query volume in dense cities.
Highly available for search; place updates can be eventually consistent.
Correct geography: must handle radius queries that cross index-cell boundaries.
Location privacy: user coordinates are sensitive — minimize and short-retain query-coordinate logs, restrict access, and keep analytics coarse-grained.
Abuse resistance: validate coordinates, cap radius and result limit, and rate-limit per client/API key on a public high-QPS endpoint.
Global reads: route users to a regional/cell-prefix index with read replicas and degrade gracefully if a regional shard is unavailable.

capacity.label: Places
capacity.note: Global; very dense in cities, sparse elsewhere.

capacity.label: Searches per second
capacity.note: Spiky around meal times and events.

capacity.label: Place updates
capacity.note: Far below reads; indexing can be async.

capacity.label: Typical radius
capacity.note: Drives how many index cells a query touches.

capacity.label: Result set
capacity.note: Ranked subset, not everything in radius.

capacity.label: Cells per query
capacity.note: Cells touched ≈ query-circle area / cell area + boundary over-cover. At ~1.2 km cells a 0.5 km radius (1 km diameter) covers about a 3x3 block (center cell + one ring, ~9 cells); fine ~150 m cells need many more (~50-60) but trim candidates per cell. The precision policy (Step 4 / final design) picks a level per region so a 0.5-5 km radius stays in the low tens of cells.

capacity.label: Candidates per query
capacity.note: Dense city cells dominate; variable precision keeps each cell's list bounded so the union stays in the low thousands before the haversine filter trims it.

capacity.label: Cache reads per query
capacity.note: Only the ranked top-K are hydrated. 10K qps x ~30 = ~300K PlaceCache reads/sec; at 90% hit rate ~30K/sec fall through to PlaceDB.

capacity.label: Geo-index size
capacity.note: cell -> place_ids for 100M places. Sharded by cell-prefix/region; hot city prefixes get dedicated shards + read replicas.

capacity.label: Hot-region skew
capacity.note: A handful of city centers carry a large share of the 10K qps, so shard sizing follows peak per-region load, not the global mean.

capacity.label: Latency budget (p99 < 200 ms)
capacity.value: ~30 geo + ~40 filter/rank + ~30 ETA (top-N only) + ~40 hydrate + ~40 network
capacity.note: Rough split in ms. ETA runs only for a small top-N with a timeout/fallback so it can't dominate; hydration is bounded by the top-K cache reads.

api.description: Find ranked places near a location within a radius, with optional filters. The gateway validates that lat∈[-90,90] / lng∈[-180,180], caps radiusM (e.g. ≤ 50 km) and limit (e.g. ≤ 50), and rate-limits per client / API key; out-of-range coordinates or oversized radius/limit are rejected with 400 / clamped. Query coordinates are logged minimally and short-retained for privacy.

Create a place (upsert by client idempotency-key). Bumps version and writes a (place_id, version) outbox event in the same transaction (drained to the change queue by a relay / CDC) that updates the geo index asynchronously; retries with the same key are no-ops.
Fetch full details for one place.

Update a place's fields (including location). A location change emits a move event carrying old_cell + new_cell so the indexer removes the old geo-index entry and adds the new one. Uses If-Match / expected version for optimistic concurrency.
Remove a place. Soft-deletes by setting status=removed (tombstone) and emits a delete event; the indexer drops it from its cell. Reads filter tombstoned ids until async cleanup completes.

dataModel.name: places
dataModel.note: Authoritative place records and lifecycle. status + tombstone let stale geo-index entries be filtered safely while async cleanup catches up; version drives idempotent indexing.

fields.name: price_level
fields.type: smallint (1-4, index) — supports the price filter

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum active|temp_closed|removed — drives open-now and tombstoning

fields.name: timezone
fields.type: varchar (IANA tz) — needed to evaluate 'open now' in local time

fields.name: source
fields.type: varchar (ingest source / owner) — provenance for conflict resolution

fields.name: version
fields.type: bigint (monotonic per place) — idempotency key for indexing

dataModel.name: geo_index
dataModel.note: Derived: cell key -> place ids, plus the minimal fields needed to filter and rank without hydrating every candidate. Backed by a spatial structure and rebuildable from places.

fields.name: cell
fields.type: varchar (geohash prefix / S2 / H3 cell id) PK

fields.name: entry_meta
fields.type: per place_id: { lat, lng, category, price_level, popularity, status, place_version, timezone, open_state } — cheap pre-rank filters live here so only the top-K hydrate full details from PlaceCache/PlaceDB. place_version makes index updates idempotent on (place_id, version); open_state is a periodically-precomputed time-bucketed open/closed/closing-soon hint (with timezone) so open-now filters without a detail fetch — status (active|temp_closed|removed) is a lifecycle flag, not the business-hours source

dataModel.name: place_change_events
dataModel.note: Change log streamed to the indexer (and the durable basis for replay/backfill). Carries old/new cell so a move reliably removes the old index entry.

fields.name: place_version
fields.type: bigint — index updates are idempotent on (place_id, version)

fields.name: old_cell
fields.type: varchar nullable — cell to remove from on a move/delete

fields.name: new_cell
fields.type: varchar nullable — cell to add to on a create/move

fields.name: event_time
fields.type: timestamp — for ordering and lag measurement

patterns.name: Geospatial indexing
patterns.what: Map 2D coordinates to a 1D cell key (geohash/S2) or tree so radius queries touch a few cells, not all rows.
patterns.whenToUse: Any 'find things near a point' query at scale.

patterns.name: Cell neighbor expansion
patterns.what: Query the covering set of cells for the radius (the target cell plus enough neighbor rings) so results near a cell boundary aren't missed; 'cell + 8 neighbors' is the small-radius special case.
patterns.whenToUse: Grid/geohash indexes where a radius can straddle cell edges.
patterns.group: Handle Cell Boundaries and the Radius

patterns.name: Adaptive cell size
patterns.what: Use finer cells in dense areas and coarser in sparse ones (tree-based or precision tuning).
patterns.whenToUse: Highly non-uniform spatial density (cities vs countryside).

patterns.name: Candidate generation + ranking
patterns.what: Cheaply gather candidates from the index, then rank/filter the smaller set.
patterns.whenToUse: When the exact, ranked result is expensive but a recall-y candidate set is cheap.

patterns.name: Async index update via change stream
patterns.what: Persist writes, emit change events, and update the derived index off the write path.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy systems where the index can lag writes by minutes.
patterns.group: Keep the Index Fresh (Async Write Path)

steps.title: 1. Naive: Scan and Compute Distance
description: Start simply: store places with lat/lng, and on a search compute the distance from the query point to every place, keeping those within the radius.
Correct, but it's a full table scan per query — O(all places) for every search. At 100M places and 10K searches/sec this is hopeless.
Even a bounding-box filter on lat/lng (two range predicates) helps only modestly and indexes poorly on two independent dimensions.

view.caption: The client's nearby query reaches the search service through the gateway, which scans the place store and computes distance to every place.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why doesn't a normal B-tree index on (lat, lng) make radius search fast?
whyNow: Establishing why naive distance scans and 2D range indexes fail is what motivates a real spatial index.

recap.newRisk: Full scan per query — won't scale at all.

traps.trap: Indexing lat and lng separately and intersecting ranges.
traps.why: A bounding box still returns a huge strip; two 1D indexes don't compose into 2D locality.
traps.instead: Map 2D space to a locality-preserving 1D cell key (geohash) or a spatial tree.

failureDrills.scenario: A client sends garbage or abusive queries — out-of-range coordinates, a 10,000 km radius, limit=1,000,000, or a flood of requests.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The system rejects or clamps the request cheaply and stays available for everyone else.
failureDrills.mitigation: Validate lat/lng bounds and reject 400s at the gateway; cap radius and limit (clamp or 400); rate-limit per client/API key so one caller can't exhaust the read path.

steps.title: 2. Geospatial Index: Geohash / S2 Cells
description: Divide the globe into a grid of cells and give each a key. Geohash interleaves lat/lng bits into a string whose prefix length sets precision; S2 maps the sphere to a quadtree of cells. Nearby points share a cell-key prefix.
Index each place by its cell key: cell -> set of place ids. A radius query computes which cells cover the area and unions their place lists — touching a handful of cells, not 100M rows.
Precompute and store each place's geohash so writes index cheaply and reads are prefix lookups.

view.caption: The search service asks the geo index for the cells in the radius to get place ids, then hydrates details from the place cache, falling back to the place store on a miss.

options.name: Geohash prefix index (default)
options.description: Interleaving latitude and longitude bits yields a string whose prefix length sets precision; lookups are prefix scans over cell-to-place-id entries.
pros: Nearby points share a common prefix, so a radius query reduces to prefix matching.
Cell -> place-id lookups are plain string-prefix scans, easy on any KV or sorted store.
Cheap to compute and store one geohash per place at write time.
cons: Fixed prefix length is one precision everywhere, so it fits neither dense nor sparse regions well.
Rectangular cells and the prime-meridian/pole seams make boundary handling awkward.
Two physically close points can fall in non-adjacent prefixes near a cell edge.

view.caption: The search service maps the query radius to geohash prefix cells in the geo index for matching place ids, then hydrates details via the place cache.

options.name: S2 cells (Hilbert-curve hierarchy)
options.description: S2 maps the sphere to a hierarchy of quadtree cells ordered along a Hilbert curve, with per-query variable cell levels covering a radius.
pros: Maps the sphere to a quadtree of cells with a 1-D Hilbert ordering, so nearby cells stay numerically close.
Per-query variable cell level covers a radius with a tight set of cells, minimizing over-fetch.
Spherical geometry avoids the lat/lng rectangle distortion and pole seams of geohash.
cons: More complex library and cell-id math than simple geohash strings.
Computing the covering cell set per query is heavier than a fixed prefix.
Operationally less familiar; harder to eyeball cell ids while debugging.

view.caption: The search service queries the geo index built on S2 cells to find places within the radius, then hydrates details through the place cache.

options.name: Quadtree (adaptive subdivision)
options.description: A quadtree subdivides space only where place density is high, so dense regions get small cells and sparse regions stay coarse.
pros: Subdivides only where density is high, so dense city cells stay small and rural cells stay coarse.
Candidate lists stay roughly bounded regardless of how clustered places are.
Tree walk naturally answers 'expand outward until enough results' for sparse areas.
cons: A dynamic tree is harder to shard and replicate than a flat cell -> ids map.
Rebalancing the tree as places are added/removed adds write-path complexity.
Deep trees mean more pointer hops per query than a single prefix lookup.

view.caption: The search service descends a quadtree geo index to the nodes covering the query area, then hydrates the matching places via the place cache.

steps.decisionPrompt: Geohash vs S2 vs a quadtree — what tradeoffs would push you to each?

concepts.definition: Interleave latitude/longitude bits into a base-32 string; prefix length controls cell size.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns 2D proximity into 1D prefix matching, which any KV/string index handles well.
concepts.example: All places in a ~150m cell share a 7-char geohash prefix.

concepts.definition: Hierarchical decomposition of space into nested cells with stable ids.
concepts.whyItMatters: Adapts cell granularity and handles sphere geometry better than a flat grid.
concepts.example: Query a covering set of S2 cells at the right level for the radius.

concepts.definition: Uber's hierarchical hexagonal cell system: uniform neighbor distances (6 neighbors, no diagonal edge case) and a tunable resolution.
concepts.whyItMatters: A practical alternative to geohash/S2 with cleaner neighbor expansion; the same covering-set idea applies.
concepts.example: k-ring(cell, 1) gives the 6 neighbors for a small-radius cover.

concepts.definition: The geo index is cell -> place_ids plus light per-entry metadata (category, price_level, popularity, status, lat/lng, place_version, timezone, open_state); rich details stay in PlaceDB/PlaceCache.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cheap filters (category/price/open-now) and the first-pass score resolve from index metadata, so only the top-K hydrate full details.
concepts.example: open-now is evaluated from the entry's precomputed open_state + timezone without a detail fetch.

whyNow: The spatial index is the heart of the problem; choosing and justifying geohash/S2 is the central senior signal.

Commit to one: introduce the geohash prefix index as the simplest baseline, then defend S2/H3 region covering (backed by a KV cell index) as the final choice because its per-query variable-level coverer gives a tighter cover for the 0.5-5 km radius range and adapts to density (Step 4) without prime-meridian/pole seams. The final design uses that S2/H3 choice consistently; geohash stays the simpler fallback.

recap.after: Radius search reads a few cells' place lists (geohash baseline → S2/H3 region covering as the defended choice).
recap.newRisk: A circle doesn't align to square cells — boundaries and false positives need handling.

steps.title: 3. Handle Cell Boundaries and the Radius
description: A search circle rarely sits inside one cell. The general rule is to compute the covering set: all cells that overlap the query circle, plus enough neighboring cells/rings for the chosen precision so a place just across a boundary isn't missed.

For a small radius on a fixed grid this reduces to the familiar 'target cell + its 8 neighbors', but that one-ring shortcut is only correct when the radius fits inside a cell. A larger radius (the dataset allows up to ~5 km) or finer cells span many cells, so the covering set can be several rings wide — compute it from the radius and cell size, don't assume one ring.
Cells are squares and the query is a circle, so the cell union over-covers: after gathering candidates, do an exact distance filter (haversine) to drop those actually outside the radius.

Pick the cell precision so the covering set stays a handful of cells for the typical radius — too coarse pulls in too many candidates per cell, too fine means querying many cells. Variable precision (Step 4) changes this covering logic: with mixed cell sizes you compute the cover at the level chosen for that region.

view.caption: The search service queries the geo index for all cells overlapping the radius (not just the center cell) and hydrates the matching places from the place cache.

steps.decisionPrompt: A cafe is 50m away but in the cell next door. How do you make sure it's returned?

concepts.definition: The set of index cells that overlap the query circle, computed from the radius and the cell precision (one ring for a small radius, several rings or coarser levels for a larger one).
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the general, correct way to find candidate cells; 'cell + 8 neighbors' is just the small-radius special case.
concepts.example: S2/H3 region coverers return a covering cell set at the level matching the radius.
concepts.group: Handle Cell Boundaries and the Radius

concepts.definition: Including adjacent cells in a spatial query to cover boundary-straddling results.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it, results near a cell edge are silently dropped — but one ring only suffices when the radius fits in a cell.
concepts.example: Query target cell + its 8 neighbors when the radius is small, then exact-distance filter.
concepts.group: Handle Cell Boundaries and the Radius

whyNow: The boundary problem is the classic follow-up; addressing it proves you understand the index isn't a magic 'within radius' oracle.

recap.after: Neighbor cells + exact filter give correct radius results.
recap.newRisk: Dense city cells can still hold huge candidate lists; sparse rural ones are nearly empty.

traps.trap: Querying only the cell the point falls in.
traps.why: Misses nearby places that happen to be in an adjacent cell.
traps.instead: Expand to neighbor cells, then exact-distance filter.

traps.trap: Assuming 'cell + 8 neighbors' always covers the requested radius.
traps.why: One neighbor ring only covers a radius that fits inside a cell. A 5 km radius, or a finer cell precision, spans many cells and several rings, so a fixed one-ring expansion silently drops places in the outer band.
traps.instead: Compute the covering set from the radius and cell size (or use an S2/H3 region coverer); treat 8-neighbors as the small-radius special case only.

flows.name: Radius query: cover cells + neighbors + exact filter
flows.note: A search computes the covering set of cells for the radius (one ring for a small radius, several for a larger one), unions their place ids, then exact-distance filters because square cells over-cover a circular radius.

messages.label: compute covering set for radius (cells + rings)
fetch place ids for those cells

steps.title: 4. Adapt to Uneven Density
description: Place density varies wildly: a downtown cell may hold thousands of places while a rural cell holds none. A single fixed cell size is wrong everywhere.

Use variable precision: in dense areas use finer cells (longer geohash / deeper quadtree level) so candidate lists stay small; in sparse areas use coarser cells so a query still finds something nearby.
Tree structures (quadtree/S2) do this naturally by subdividing only where density is high; with geohash you adjust prefix length by region or expand outward until enough results are found.

Mixed precision needs explicit query rules: pick the level(s) covering the radius for that region; when the cover spans both parent and child cells, dedupe candidates so a place indexed at a child level isn't also pulled in via its parent; and cap fanout (max cells / max candidates) so a pathological query can't touch the whole tree — fall back to a coarser level or expanding rings when the cap is hit.

Concrete targets to defend: aim for ~50-100 candidates per cell and a covering set under ~60 cells, with a hard cap (e.g. max 64 cells / max ~2,000 raw candidates) per query. If the cover would exceed the cap (a huge radius over a dense metro), step to a coarser level or stop expanding rings and return a best-effort top-K rather than fanning out unbounded.

view.caption: The search service queries the geo index, whose cell sizing adapts to place density so each cell holds a manageable number of results.

options.name: Variable-precision cells (default)
options.description: Index granularity varies by region — fine cells in dense areas, coarse cells in sparse ones — so candidate lists stay bounded everywhere.
pros: Finer cells in dense downtowns keep candidate lists small; coarser cells in rural areas still return something.
Tree structures (quadtree/S2) subdivide only where density is high, adapting automatically.
Matches index granularity to actual place distribution rather than a single global guess.
cons: Mixed cell sizes complicate the 'query my cell + neighbors' logic.
With geohash you must pick prefix length per region or expand outward, adding query logic.
Region-specific precision tuning needs density stats and ongoing maintenance.

view.caption: The search service reads variable-precision cells from the geo index, so dense areas use finer cells and sparse areas coarser ones.

options.name: Fixed cell size everywhere
options.description: Every cell uses one fixed precision across the whole map, so indexing, sharding, and neighbor logic stay uniform.
pros: Simplest possible scheme: one precision, uniform cells, trivial to index and shard.
Boundary and neighbor logic is uniform since every cell is the same size.
No per-region tuning or density statistics to maintain.
cons: A dense Manhattan cell returns thousands of candidates, blowing the per-query budget.
A rural Montana cell may be empty, returning nothing within the radius.
No single size is right everywhere, so it fails at both extremes.

view.caption: The search service queries a geo index with one fixed cell size everywhere, regardless of how dense or sparse the area is.

options.name: Expanding-ring search (fixed cells, grow radius)
options.description: Cells stay fine and fixed, but the query expands outward ring by ring until it gathers K results.
pros: Keep fine fixed cells but query progressively wider rings until K results are found.
Sparse areas just expand a few rings, so users still get nearby results.
Dense areas stop after the first ring, keeping candidate counts small.
cons: Multiple sequential index lookups per query raise latency in sparse regions.
Stopping condition (how many is 'enough') needs careful tuning.
Worst case in empty regions touches many rings before giving up.

view.caption: The search service queries fixed-size cells in the geo index ring by ring, expanding the radius outward until it has found K results.

links.label: query rings outward until K found

steps.decisionPrompt: Manhattan vs rural Montana with the same cell size — what breaks in each?

concepts.definition: Varying cell granularity with local density.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps candidate counts bounded in cities while still returning results in sparse areas.
concepts.example: Quadtree subdivides a busy block into many cells; leaves a rural region as one.

whyNow: Real geography is non-uniform; handling density is what makes the index perform consistently, not just on average.

recap.after: Cell granularity tracks density; bounded candidate counts.
recap.newRisk: Variable cells complicate neighbor logic; ranking still needed to pick the best of many candidates.

traps.trap: Querying parent and child cells together without deduping.
traps.why: With mixed precision a place can be reachable via both a coarse parent and a finer child cell, so the candidate union double-counts it.
traps.instead: Pick one consistent level per region for the cover, or dedupe place ids across levels before filtering.

steps.title: 5. Rank and Filter Candidates
description: The index gives candidates (recall); now produce the ordered result the user wants. Rank in stages so cost stays bounded. Stage 1 applies cheap filters (category, price, open-now) and a blended distance/rating/popularity score using fields carried in the geo index, with no per-candidate hydration.

Score remaining candidates by a blend of exact distance, rating, and popularity — distance alone isn't what users want (a great cafe 400m away beats a mediocre one next door). Most filters resolve from index/entry metadata; open-now reads the entry's precomputed open_state + timezone (refreshed periodically from the place's normalized hours) rather than hydrating every candidate.

Stage 2 runs expensive signals — routing/ETA or an ML re-rank — only over a small top-N (e.g. top 20-50), never every candidate. The ETA call has a tight timeout and falls back to straight-line distance, so the routing service can never blow the p99 budget. Travel distance is approximated or cached for ranking; full turn-by-turn routing is computed only when the UI asks for directions.

Hydrate full details for the top-K from a hot place cache (cache-aside to the place store) so only the results you return cost a detail lookup. Track ranker and ETA p95/p99 separately from overall search p99 so a slow optional stage is visible.

view.caption: After the geo index returns candidates, the search service ranks them via the ranking service and the routing/ETA service, then hydrates details from the place cache.

options.name: Blended score: distance + rating + popularity (default)
options.description: Candidates are filtered first, then scored by a weighted blend of distance, rating, and popularity, and only the top-K are hydrated.
pros: A great cafe 400m away can beat a mediocre one next door — matches what users actually want.
Filters (category, open-now, price) shrink the set before scoring, so ranking stays cheap.
Only the top-K are hydrated from the place cache, bounding detail-lookup cost.
cons: Hand-tuned weights across distance, rating, and popularity are subjective and drift over time.
Popularity bias can bury good new or niche places.
Combining heterogeneous signals into one score needs careful normalization.

view.caption: The search service sends geo-index candidates to the ranking service for a blended distance-plus-rating-plus-popularity score, using the ETA service for travel distance.

options.name: Rank by raw distance only
options.description: Candidates are simply sorted by haversine distance to the query point, nearest first, with no quality signals.
pros: Dead simple and fully explainable: nearest first, no weights to tune.
No rating/popularity data needed, so it works on a bare place index.
Deterministic and fast — just sort candidates by haversine distance.
cons: Ignores quality: a closed or poorly-rated place outranks a great one slightly farther away.
Doesn't reflect what users pick (they trade a little distance for quality).
No way to surface popular or sponsored results.

view.caption: The search service orders the geo-index candidates purely by raw distance and hydrates them from the place cache, with no separate ranker.

options.name: Learned ranking model (ML re-rank)
options.description: A model trained on click and visit logs scores candidates, learning from data how users weigh distance, rating, and context.
pros: A model learns from clicks/visits how users actually weigh distance, rating, and context.
Captures interactions (time of day, query intent) that hand-tuned weights miss.
Improves over time as more interaction data accrues.
cons: Adds a feature pipeline, training, and a model-serving hop to the query path.
Inference latency and infra cost are far higher than a simple weighted sort.
Needs substantial interaction logs and guards against feedback-loop bias.

view.caption: The search service scores geo-index candidates with a learned ranking model, drawing travel distance from the ETA service before hydrating details.

steps.decisionPrompt: Should you rank by raw distance, or something else? What signals matter?
whyNow: Separating cheap candidate generation from a richer ranking pass is the standard search architecture and keeps the index simple.

concepts.definition: A cheap recall stage that gathers candidates, then an expensive precision stage that orders them.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the hot index lookup simple while allowing rich, evolving ranking.
concepts.example: Geo index returns 500 candidates; the ranker scores and returns the top 20.

recap.newRisk: Ranking adds latency; index must stay fresh as places change.

traps.trap: Hydrating full details for every candidate before filtering and ranking.
traps.why: Dense cells can return hundreds of candidates; a detail lookup per candidate multiplies cache/DB load and breaks the bounded-cost story.
traps.instead: Filter and score from index/entry metadata first; hydrate full details only for the top-K you return.

traps.trap: Calling the routing/ETA service for every candidate.
traps.why: Per-candidate ETA fanout turns a geo-search into a slow routing service and dominates the p99 budget.
traps.instead: Run ETA/ML only over a small top-N with a timeout and a straight-line-distance fallback.

failureDrills.scenario: The ETA/routing service is slow or down.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Search still returns ranked results on time.
failureDrills.mitigation: Tight per-call timeout; fall back to straight-line distance and rank without ETA; track ETA p99 separately.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: User still gets a reasonable ordering.
failureDrills.mitigation: Fall back to the cheap stage-1 score (distance + rating) and serve the top-K from that.

failureDrills.scenario: PlaceCache is down so details can't be hydrated.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Results still return, possibly with leaner detail.
failureDrills.mitigation: Fall back to PlaceDB reads for the bounded top-K (capacity-limited); shed optional fields rather than fail the query.

flows.name: Candidates -> filter -> rank -> hydrate
flows.note: Cheap recall from the index, then filter, score by distance/rating/popularity, and hydrate full details for only the top-K from the place cache.

steps.title: 6. Keep the Index Fresh (Async Write Path)
description: Place writes go to the authoritative store; the change event {place_id, event_id, place_version, op, old_cell, new_cell, event_time} must be produced atomically with that write — not as a best-effort app publish after commit, which can lose the event if the process dies between commit and publish. The default is a transactional outbox: write the place row and an outbox row in one PlaceDB transaction, then a relay drains the outbox to the change queue; CDC off the place/change table is the equivalent log-based alternative. Either way the event is created if and only if the write committed.
An indexer consumes events and upserts the affected geo cells — removing old_cell and adding new_cell on a move.

The geo index is derived state, so correctness comes from replayability, not from the queue alone. Make index updates idempotent keyed by (place_id, version): apply an event only if its version is newer than what the cell already reflects, so duplicate or out-of-order deliveries are safe. Park poison events in a dead-letter queue and replay/backfill from the authoritative PlaceDB.

Support a full rebuild from PlaceDB: build a fresh index into a shadow namespace, verify counts/lag, then atomically swap it into service (alias flip) so reads never see a half-built index. Partial reconciliation jobs periodically diff cells against PlaceDB to repair drift.

Decoupling indexing from the write path keeps writes fast and lets the read-heavy index scale independently; the index may lag writes by minutes, which the requirements allow. Track that lag explicitly: queue lag, index age by region/cell-prefix, stale-result rate, and rebuild progress, with SLOs and alerts.

Shard the geo index by cell-key prefix (region) so dense regions live on dedicated shards; replicate read replicas for query volume, and split hot cells when a single cell's place list grows too large.

Globally, cell-prefix is the ownership key: a query routes to the shard owning the covering cells (usually the user's own region), reading from a nearby read replica. If a regional shard is unavailable, degrade gracefully — serve a slightly staler replica or a coarser/best-effort result rather than failing the search. Cross-region writes still go to the authoritative store and re-key cells; only the derived index is regionalized, so there is one source of truth, not conflicting regional masters.

view.caption: When a place changes, the place service writes the place store and emits a change event; the change queue delivers it to the index builder, which upserts the geo index.

options.name: Async index via change queue (default)
options.description: Place writes hit the authoritative store, then a change event flows through a queue to an indexer that updates the geo index asynchronously.
pros: Place writes hit the authoritative store fast; a change event updates the geo index off the write path.
Read-heavy index scales independently and can be re-sharded/replicated for query volume.
Minutes of index lag is acceptable per the requirements, and the queue absorbs write bursts.
cons: The index is eventually consistent, so a just-added place may not appear for minutes.
Adds a queue + indexer to operate and monitor for lag.
Lost/duplicated events need idempotent upserts and replay to stay correct.

view.caption: The place service emits each change to the place change queue, and the index builder consumes events to upsert geo-index cell entries asynchronously.

options.name: Synchronous dual write on the write path
options.description: A single request synchronously writes both the place store and the geo index together, so a place is searchable immediately.
pros: A new or moved place is searchable immediately — no index lag.
Fewer moving parts: no queue or separate indexer to run.
Simple mental model: one request updates store and index together.
cons: Every place write now pays geo-index latency, slowing the write path.
Store and index can diverge if the second write fails mid-request (no atomic 2-write).
Write path and read-heavy index are coupled, so they can't scale independently.

view.caption: The place service writes the place store and upserts the geo-index cell inline in the same request, keeping index and store in sync synchronously.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why update the geo index asynchronously instead of synchronously on each place write?

concepts.definition: Writing the change event in the same transaction as the place row (an outbox table a relay drains), or capturing it from the DB log via CDC, instead of publishing best-effort after commit.
concepts.whyItMatters: Closes the dual-write hole: the event exists if and only if the write committed, so the derived index can't silently miss a place.
concepts.example: PlaceDB txn writes places + outbox; a relay forwards outbox rows to ChangeQ at-least-once.
concepts.group: Keep the Index Fresh (Async Write Path)

concepts.definition: Applying a change event keyed by (place_id, version) so reprocessing the same or an out-of-order event has no extra effect.
concepts.whyItMatters: Queues deliver at-least-once; without version-keyed idempotency replays would corrupt cell membership.
concepts.example: Skip an event whose version <= the version already reflected in the cell.
concepts.group: Keep the Index Fresh (Async Write Path)

concepts.definition: Building a fresh index from the source of truth into a shadow namespace and flipping an alias to it once verified.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you recover from drift or schema changes without serving a half-built index.
concepts.example: Reindex all of PlaceDB into geo_v2, verify, then point reads at geo_v2.
concepts.group: Keep the Index Fresh (Async Write Path)

patterns: Async index update via change stream
whyNow: Tying the write path to async indexing closes the loop: reads stay fast, the index scales separately, and freshness is a tunable lag.

recap.after: Index updated off the write path; change events created atomically with the write via outbox/CDC; idempotent on (place_id, version); sharded by region; rebuildable via atomic swap; lag observable.
recap.newRisk: Index lag; a move re-keys a place across cells (delete-from-old + add-to-new must be atomic-ish); needs lag SLOs and dead-letter handling.

bottlenecks.issue: A dense-region index shard gets hammered by city queries.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Finer cells + dedicated shard + read replicas for hot regions; split a hot cell whose place list grows too large.

bottlenecks.issue: A place moving location lingers in its old cell.
bottlenecks.mitigation: The move event carries old_cell + new_cell; the indexer removes from old and adds to new idempotently on (place_id, version).

bottlenecks.issue: PlaceCache serves stale details after a place update (renamed, hours changed, closed).
bottlenecks.mitigation: On the change event, invalidate or refresh the cached detail by place_id; use a short TTL as a backstop and filter tombstoned places at read time.

failureDrills.scenario: The indexer falls behind during a bulk import.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads serve slightly stale results; the queue absorbs the backlog and catches up.
failureDrills.mitigation: Eventually-consistent index + scalable consumers; alert on queue-lag/index-age SLOs and bound the acceptable lag.

failureDrills.scenario: The place row commits but the change event never reaches the queue.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The index still converges — no place is silently missing because of a lost publish.
failureDrills.mitigation: Produce the event in the same transaction as the write (outbox table) or via CDC, so commit and event are atomic; a relay retries delivery, and reconciliation against PlaceDB repairs any residual drift.

failureDrills.scenario: A change event is delivered twice or out of order.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The index stays correct — a stale or duplicate event is a no-op.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent upserts keyed by (place_id, version); apply only if the event version is newer than the cell's recorded version.

failureDrills.scenario: A poison event repeatedly fails to apply.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It stops blocking the stream and is investigated out of band.
failureDrills.mitigation: Dead-letter the event after N retries; replay/backfill the affected place from PlaceDB.

failureDrills.scenario: A regional geo-index shard (or its replicas) becomes unavailable.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Searches in that region still return, possibly slightly staler or coarser, rather than erroring.
failureDrills.mitigation: Read from a surviving replica or fail over to a coarser/global fallback index; the authoritative PlaceDB is unaffected, so the shard rebuilds from it on recovery.

failureDrills.scenario: The geo index is corrupted or its schema must change.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads keep serving from the current index while a clean one is built.
failureDrills.mitigation: Full rebuild from PlaceDB into a shadow namespace, verify, then atomic alias swap; reconciliation jobs repair drift continuously.

flows.name: Place change -> async geo-index update
flows.note: A write persists to the authoritative store and emits a change event; the indexer re-keys the place across geo cells off the write path.

messages.label: remove old cell, add new cell

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Geo-Indexed Nearby Search
finalDesign.description: Places persist to an authoritative store and produce (place_id, version) change events atomically with the write via a transactional outbox / CDC; an indexer maintains a sharded geospatial index — the defended default is S2/H3 region covering backed by a KV cell index (geohash prefixes are the simpler baseline) — mapping cells to place ids with light per-entry filter metadata (category, price_level, open_state, timezone, place_version). A search resolves a location+radius into a covering set of cells, gathers candidate place ids, exact-distance filters, cheaply filters/scores from index metadata, runs ETA/ML only over a small top-N with a timeout fallback, and hydrates the top-K from a hot place cache. Cell granularity adapts to density with a fanout cap. The gateway validates coordinates and caps radius/limit and rate-limits per client/API key; coordinate logs are minimized and short-retained. Reads route by region/cell-prefix to read replicas and degrade gracefully if a regional shard is unavailable. Indexing is async and idempotent on (place_id, version), with dead-letter + replay/backfill from PlaceDB, full rebuild via shadow-namespace atomic swap, and lag/index-age SLOs by region so reads stay fast and the derived index stays correct.

view.caption: The client queries the search service, which reads cells from the geo index, ranks via the ranking and ETA services, and hydrates from the place cache; place writes flow through the place service and change queue into the index builder that keeps the geo index fresh.

flows.name: Search flow: request -> cells -> candidates -> filter/rank -> hydrate
flows.note: End-to-end read path: resolve the radius to a covering set of cells, fetch candidates, exact-distance filter, cheaply filter/rank from index metadata (ETA only for the top-N with a fallback), then hydrate the top-K from the place cache.

flows.name: Write flow: create/update/delete -> event -> idempotent index update
flows.note: End-to-end write path: persist the authoritative record and bump version, emit a (place_id, version) event with old/new cell, and have the indexer apply it idempotently so the change becomes query-visible.

messages.label: persist + bump version + outbox {place_id, version, op, old_cell, new_cell} (one txn; tombstone on delete)

functional.how: Covering set of cells for the radius + exact-distance (haversine) filter.
Cheap filters applied before ranking from geo_index.entry_meta (category, price_level); open-now from the entry's precomputed open_state + timezone, no per-candidate hydration.
Ranking service blends distance, rating, and popularity over the candidate set (ETA only for the top-N).
Change events update the geo index asynchronously, within the minutes-scale lag the requirements allow.

nonFunctional.how: Few-cell index lookup + bounded candidates + cached details.
Index sharded by region; read replicas for query volume.
Adaptive cell granularity bounds candidate counts everywhere.
Read path independent of writes; eventually-consistent index.

Change events created atomically with the write (transactional outbox / CDC), idempotent (place_id, version) updates, dead-letter + replay/backfill, and full rebuild via shadow-namespace atomic swap with lag/index-age SLOs.

technologyChoices.concern: API edge, coordinate validation, and abuse controls
tradeoff: The edge should reject invalid coordinates, cap radius/result limits, and enforce per-client/API-key rate limits before hot geo shards see traffic.
A managed edge reduces operational work for TLS, WAF rules, quotas, and request logs, but application-level validation still protects internal callers and bypass paths.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed API edge with rate limiting and WAF policies removes most custom reverse-proxy glue for public nearby-search endpoints.

technologyChoices.concern: Authoritative place store and transactional outbox
tradeoff: A relational place store makes lifecycle, versioning, idempotency keys, and outbox writes easy to keep in one transaction.
Document or key-value stores scale writes and flexible metadata well, but the app must enforce old-cell/new-cell move semantics and event atomicity carefully.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A database-native CDC feed or transactional outbox removes the unsafe dual-write pattern where a place update commits but its indexing event is lost.

selfHosted.name: S2 / H3 cell ids over Redis/Valkey or a sorted KV

tradeoff: PostGIS/Elasticsearch give rich geo queries out of the box but you operate and shard them; raw S2/H3 over a KV is leaner and cheaper at scale but you build covering-set and neighbor logic yourself.
A managed geo-capable DB removes index sizing/sharding toil at higher cost and less control over hot-cell splitting.
makesIrrelevant: A managed auto-sharding geo store can remove hand-rolled cell-prefix sharding for many scales.

technologyChoices.concern: Cell covering and adaptive precision library
tradeoff: S2/H3-style coverers make radius and boundary handling explicit and support density-aware levels, but the service owns fanout limits and neighbor expansion.
Database-native spatial functions are simpler to adopt, but large hot regions can force careful partitioning and query planning.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A robust covering library removes brittle hand-authored neighbor tables and one-off boundary cases in the search service.

technologyChoices.concern: Search service runtime and regional read scaling
tradeoff: Stateless regional search workers scale horizontally and keep p99 bounded when they route to local cell-prefix shards.
Global active-active reads improve availability and latency, but the design must tolerate stale regional replicas and degraded shards.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Provider-managed global load balancing and autoscaling remove much of the custom regional router and capacity-drain logic.

tradeoff: A simple deterministic ranker is transparent and fast for distance, rating, popularity, open-now, and category filters.
A learned ranker can improve relevance, but it must run only on the bounded candidate set and have a timeout/fallback so it does not dominate p99.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A search platform with built-in geo distance sorting and filters can remove some custom rank-stage code, but it does not remove exact-distance trimming or top-K hydration.

tradeoff: CDC off the place store guarantees no missed change without app-level dual-write, but couples to the DB; an app-emitted event is simpler but must be transactional with the write (outbox pattern).
A log (Kafka/Kinesis) makes replay/backfill and ordering natural; a plain queue is simpler but needs explicit replay tooling.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention, consumer lag metrics, and DLQ integration removes much of the hand-built replay and offset bookkeeping.

technologyChoices.concern: Indexer workers, replay, and shadow rebuilds
tradeoff: Long-running workers are a good fit for continuous indexing with backpressure, lag metrics, and idempotent retries.
Batch or workflow tooling is better for full rebuilds and shadow-namespace swaps because it makes progress, checkpoints, and rollback visible.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed batch/stream workers can remove custom rebuild schedulers, but not the version checks that keep index writes idempotent.

tradeoff: A managed cache removes failover/scaling toil; self-hosted Redis gives more control over eviction and geo-replication.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A highly available managed cache removes most custom cache failover work, but the service still needs cache-miss bounds and stale-detail handling.

tradeoff: A managed routing API is fast to adopt but adds per-call cost and an external dependency on the hot path; self-hosted OSRM/Valhalla is cheaper per call but you host map data and tiles.
Either way, call only for the top-N with a timeout and a straight-line fallback so it can't dominate p99.
makesIrrelevant: Caching/approximating travel distance can remove most live routing calls for ranking; live routing then only runs when the user opens directions.

technologyChoices.concern: Coordinate privacy, logs, and coarse analytics
tradeoff: Precise coordinates are useful for debugging ranking and coverage, but they are sensitive and should be minimized, short-retained, encrypted, and access-controlled.
Aggregating to coarse cells supports traffic and capacity analysis without retaining user-level trails.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Strict log redaction and cell-level analytics remove the need for broad access to raw coordinate logs in normal operations.

technologyChoices.concern: Geo index observability and SLO dashboards
tradeoff: Nearby search needs region/cell-prefix metrics such as p99 latency, candidate fanout, cache hit rate, queue lag, index age, and stale-result filters.
Managed observability suites reduce setup work, but the application still needs domain metrics that generic infrastructure dashboards do not infer.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Good index-age and cell-hotspot dashboards remove many manual audit scripts used to discover stale shards after incidents.

say: Confirm it's read-heavy proximity search with ranking, not exact GIS routing.
Agree the index may lag writes by minutes (eventual consistency).
Show the naive scan to motivate a spatial index.
Handle boundaries with neighbor cells + exact filter.
Async index maintenance via change stream; shard by region.
Tradeoffs: cell-size tuning, index lag, dense-region hotspots.
Operational closure: idempotent events, replay/backfill, full rebuild via atomic swap, lag/index-age SLOs.
Validation & abuse: cap radius and result limits, validate coordinates, rate-limit per client/API key.
Location privacy: minimize and short-retain user-coordinate logs, restrict access.

expectations: May miss boundary and density issues.
Handles boundaries with neighbor expansion + exact filter.
Reasons about adaptive precision and hot-region sharding.
Discusses geohash vs S2 vs quadtree tradeoffs.
Extends cleanly to moving objects and ETA-aware ranking.

followUps: How would you extend this to moving objects (e.g. nearby drivers updating location every few seconds)?
Geohash vs S2 vs quadtree — when would you pick each in production?
How do you handle a place that changes location (re-keying across cells)?
How would you add ETA-aware ranking using a routing service without blowing the latency budget?
How do you keep dense-city index shards from becoming hotspots?
How do you rebuild the geo index from scratch (or after corruption) without serving a half-built index?
How do you protect user-location privacy — log minimization, retention, and access controls for coordinates?
How do you defend the search endpoint from abuse — radius caps, result limits, and per-client rate limits?

links.groupDescription: Grid systems and covering mechanics for radius queries, boundary handling, and uneven spatial density.
links.title: H3 Documentation
links.why: Reference for H3's hierarchical hexagonal cells, resolutions, and index operations.

links.groupDescription: Grid systems and covering mechanics for radius queries, boundary handling, and uneven spatial density.
links.title: Grid traversal functions
links.why: Shows gridDisk/gridRing-style neighbor expansion, which maps directly to covering nearby cells around a query point.

links.groupDescription: Grid systems and covering mechanics for radius queries, boundary handling, and uneven spatial density.

links.why: Explains why Uber adopted hierarchical hex cells for marketplace analysis, neighbor lookup, and density-aware aggregation.

links.groupDescription: Grid systems and covering mechanics for radius queries, boundary handling, and uneven spatial density.
links.title: S2 Geometry
links.why: Spherical geometry and cell hierarchy reference for building globe-scale spatial indexes without planar edge cases.

links.groupDescription: Grid systems and covering mechanics for radius queries, boundary handling, and uneven spatial density.
links.title: S2 Covering Examples
links.why: Demonstrates covering regions with S2 cells, including the precision/fanout tradeoff behind radius-query cell sets.

links.why: Spatial database reference for geospatial queries, indexing, and geometry operations.

links.groupDescription: Database and search-engine implementations of indexed geospatial filtering, sorting, and retrieval.

links.why: Shows an index-aware distance predicate for finding records within a radius without scanning every row.
